matter.title: Harbor Outfitters LLC v. Coastal Provisions LLC et al.
matter.summary: Harbor Outfitters LLC sues its supplier Coastal Provisions LLC and the supplier's owner, Maria Q. Jessup, for breach of a master supply agreement covering specialty food products, alleging delivery of defective goods that left $264326 owing, and seeks to enforce Maria's personal guaranty.

fact_pattern.summary: Harbor Outfitters LLC sues its supplier Coastal Provisions LLC and the supplier's owner, Maria Q. Jessup, for breach of a master supply agreement covering specialty food products, alleging delivery of defective goods that left $264326 owing, and seeks to enforce Maria's personal guaranty.
fact_pattern.narrative:
On or about 2024-01-17, Harbor Outfitters LLC and Coastal Provisions LLC entered into a master supply agreement under which Coastal Provisions LLC would supply specialty food products for distribution by Harbor Outfitters LLC. The agreement was valued at roughly $2515804 over its term, and Maria Q. Jessup, the supplier's owner, personally guaranteed Coastal Provisions LLC's performance and payment obligations.

Beginning around 2025-02-07, the relationship broke down over delivery of defective goods.  alleges the conduct breached the agreement and the implied warranties of merchantability and fitness, leaving approximately $264326 unpaid or unrecovered.  served a written notice of default on 2025-06-16 and, when the dispute was not resolved, covered by buying substitute goods from another supplier to keep its own customers supplied.

Harbor Outfitters LLC filed suit in Sagadahoc County Superior Court asserting breach of contract, breach of warranty, and account stated against Coastal Provisions LLC, and a separate count to enforce the personal guaranty against Maria Q. Jessup.  denies liability and has counterclaimed. The matter is now in active, contested discovery.
undisputed_facts: Harbor Outfitters LLC and Coastal Provisions LLC signed a master supply agreement on or about 2024-01-17.
Maria Q. Jessup signed a personal guaranty of Coastal Provisions LLC's obligations under the agreement.
Harbor Outfitters LLC served a written notice of default dated 2025-06-16.
disputed_facts: Whether Coastal Provisions LLC's conduct amounted to delivery of defective goods or whether the goods and performance conformed to the agreement.
The amount, if any, owed to Harbor Outfitters LLC, alleged to be about $264326.
Whether Harbor Outfitters LLC reasonably mitigated its damages through its cover purchases.
Whether the personal guaranty is enforceable against Maria Q. Jessup.

timeline.event: Harbor Outfitters LLC and Coastal Provisions LLC executed a master supply agreement; Maria signed the guaranty.
The alleged breach (delivery of defective goods) occurred.
served a written notice of default.
Complaint filed in Bath Superior Court.

third_parties.name: Evergreen Builders Inc.
third_parties.relevance: Carried the shipments and documents the timing and condition of deliveries.

third_parties.name: Nadia K. Greenhill
third_parties.relevance: Has direct knowledge of the contract negotiations and the alleged quality problems.

third_parties.name: Nadia S. Loe
third_parties.relevance: Was the plaintiff's day-to-day contact and negotiated the orders and delivery schedule.

third_parties.name: Cobblestone Studios Corp.
third_parties.relevance: The competing supplier the plaintiff turned to in mitigation after the breach; its invoices show the cover price.

exchanges.question: What was the arrangement with the supplier?
exchanges.answer: We had a master supply agreement for specialty food products, worth around $2515804 over its term. Maria, who owns the company, signed a personal guaranty, which is why we were comfortable extending credit.

exchanges.question: What went wrong?
exchanges.answer: It came down to delivery of defective goods. By 2025-02-07 it was clear we had a real problem, and we are out roughly $264326. We sent a formal notice of default on 2025-06-16.

exchanges.question: What did you do to limit the damage?
exchanges.answer: We could not leave our own customers hanging, so we sourced substitute goods from another supplier at a higher price. We kept every invoice so we can show the cover cost.

exchanges.question: How important is the personal guaranty to you?
exchanges.answer: Very. The company may not be good for the money, so Maria's guaranty is how we actually get paid. I want to hold Maria to it.

exchanges.question: They are claiming you breached. What is your view?
exchanges.answer: They say we wrongfully rejected the goods, but the goods were defective and we documented it. Their counterclaim is a pressure tactic and I want it defeated.

exchanges.question: How do you want this to end?
exchanges.answer: Recover the debt and our cover costs, get a judgment against the guarantor, and beat the counterclaim. If they make a fair offer, I will settle rather than run up fees.

goals: Recover the approximately $264326 owed plus cover and lost-profit damages from Coastal Provisions LLC.
Enforce the personal guaranty and obtain a judgment against Maria Q. Jessup as guarantor.
Defeat Coastal Provisions LLC's counterclaim for breach and wrongful rejection.
priorities: Reaching the guarantor as a real source of recovery.
Building a clean record on damages and mitigation.
Resolving the case efficiently if a fair settlement is available.
desired_outcomes: A judgment or settlement for the debt plus cover and lost-profit damages, with prejudgment interest and fees.
Personal liability of Maria Q. Jessup under the guaranty.
non_starters: Any resolution that releases the guarantor without paying the core debt.

constraints.budget: Cost-conscious; wants staged budgets and an early, realistic settlement assessment.
constraints.timeline: Wants the case trial-ready within roughly eighteen months of filing.

issues.issue: Recovery of cover and mitigation damages
sub_questions: Did the buyer cover in good faith and without unreasonable delay?
What is the difference between the cover price and the contract price?
issues.client_position: The cover purchases were reasonable and measure the recoverable damages.

sub_questions: From what date does prejudgment interest run on the amounts owed?

issues.issue: Whether Coastal Provisions LLC breached the supply/distribution agreement
sub_questions: Was there a valid, enforceable agreement?
Did the defendant's conduct constitute a material breach?
issues.client_position: The agreement was valid and the defendant materially breached it.

issues.issue: Enforceability of the personal guaranty against the owner
sub_questions: Is the guaranty supported by consideration and properly signed?
Are there suretyship defenses that discharge the guarantor?
issues.client_position: The guaranty is enforceable and the guarantor is liable for the debt.

expert_opinions.topic: Whether the delivered goods conformed to specification
expert_opinions.opinion: The goods met the applicable specifications and were fit for their ordinary purpose.
expert_opinions.basis: Inspection records, specifications, and sample testing.

expert_opinions.topic: Trade usage and customary terms in the relevant supply market
expert_opinions.opinion: The defendant's performance fell short of customary standards and the parties' course of dealing.
expert_opinions.basis: Industry practice, the agreement, and order history.

expert_opinions.topic: Damages, lost profits, and the cover differential
expert_opinions.opinion: The plaintiff's recoverable loss, including cover and lost profits, approximates the amount claimed.
expert_opinions.basis: Invoices, the account ledger, cover-purchase records, and profit margins.

evidence.title: Executed supply/distribution agreement
evidence.description: The signed contract governing the parties' supply relationship.

evidence.title: Personal guaranty
evidence.description: The guaranty signed by the owner of the supplier.

evidence.title: Unpaid invoices and account ledger
evidence.description: The outstanding invoices and running account statement showing the balance due.

evidence.title: Inspection and rejection records
evidence.description: Receiving, inspection, and rejection paperwork for the disputed goods.

evidence.title: Email negotiations and order confirmations
evidence.description: The email record of the negotiations, purchase orders, and confirmations.

evidence.title: Notice of default
evidence.description: The written notice of default sent to the supplier and guarantor.

evidence.title: Proof of cover purchases
evidence.description: Invoices from the substitute supplier documenting the cover price.

evidence.title: Defendant's financial statements
evidence.description: The supplier's financials, relevant to collectability and the counterclaim.

amounts.label: Amount owed / claimed
amounts.basis: Unpaid invoices and the balance on the account stated.

amounts.label: Prejudgment interest
amounts.basis: Statutory prejudgment interest on the amounts owed. 14 M.R.S. § 1602-B.

amounts.label: Attorney fees and costs
amounts.basis: Estimated plaintiff-side fees and costs through trial.

causes_of_action.relief_sought: Compensatory damages of approximately $264326 plus cover and lost-profit damages

causes_of_action.claim: Breach of the implied warranties of merchantability and fitness
elements: A sale of goods by a merchant
Goods that were not merchantable or fit for purpose
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Damages for the defective goods and consequential loss

elements: A promise, express or implied, to pay
causes_of_action.relief_sought: Judgment for the agreed unpaid balance
Judgment against the guarantor for the guaranteed debt

affirmative_defenses.basis: The plaintiff's cover purchases were unreasonable and inflated the claimed loss.
Any amount owed is offset by the plaintiff's own breaches and chargebacks.
The claims accrued more than four years before filing. 11 M.R.S. § 2-725.
The parties resolved the disputed amounts by a later agreement and payment.

counterclaims.claim: Breach of contract by the plaintiff
counterclaims.relief: Damages for the plaintiff's nonpayment and failure to take agreed quantities.
Damages for the price of goods wrongfully rejected and resale losses.

discovery.description: Admissions on the receipt of invoices and the balance due.
Requests for the defendant's financial statements and accounting records.
Subpoena to the bank for payment and receivable records.
First set of interrogatories on the deliveries, defects, and account.
Deposition of the account manager who handled the orders.

motions.title: Motion for partial summary judgment on the guaranty
motions.relief: Judgment that the personal guaranty is enforceable against the guarantor.

motions.title: Motion for a protective order
motions.relief: Protection of confidential pricing and customer information.

motions.title: Motion to dismiss for failure to state a claim
motions.relief: Dismissal of the warranty and account-stated counts.

docket.entry: Complaint and demand for jury trial filed.
Scheduling order entered; discovery deadline set.
Order denying the motion to dismiss.
Order compelling production of the defendant's financials.
Cross-motions for summary judgment on the guaranty briefed.
Complaint for breach of contract and unjust enrichment filed and served.
Defendant served its Answer and a Counterclaim alleging the plaintiff breached first and failed to mitigate.
Defendant moved for summary judgment under M.R. Civ. P. 56, with a statement of material facts.

procedure.event: Complaint for breach of contract and unjust enrichment filed and served.
Defendant served its Answer and a Counterclaim alleging the plaintiff breached first and failed to mitigate.
procedure.deadline_rule: M.R. Civ. P. 7(a) / 12(a) — reply to a counterclaim within 21 days.

procedure.event: Defendant moved for summary judgment under M.R. Civ. P. 56, with a statement of material facts.
procedure.deadline_rule: M.R. Civ. P. 56(c) / 7(b) — opposition and opposing statement of material facts within 21 days.

facts.relief_requested: compensatory damages, prejudgment interest, and costs
